docs.title: Welcome to Vilnius 🇱🇹
docs.text:
Vilnius is the capital and largest city of Lithuania — a hidden gem of Europe where medieval cobblestones meet a vibrant modern culture. Founded in the 14th century, Vilnius boasts one of the largest surviving medieval Old Towns in Europe, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1994.

Quick Facts

  • Population: ~580,000 (city proper)
  • Language: Lithuanian (English widely spoken in tourist areas)
  • Currency: Euro (€)
  • Time Zone: EET (UTC+2), EEST (UTC+3 in summer)
  • Best time to visit: May–September

docs.title: What You'll Find Here
docs.text:
🗺️ Travel Guide

Everything you need before you arrive — visas, transport, budgeting, and practical tips.

Get started → 🏘️ Districts

Explore Vilnius neighbourhood by neighbourhood — from the Old Town to bohemian Užupis.

Explore districts → 🍽️ Food & Drink

Discover Lithuanian cuisine, the best restaurants, and the city's thriving coffee scene.

Discover food →

docs.title: Why Visit Vilnius?
docs.text:
Vilnius offers an incredible mix of experiences that punch well above its weight for a European capital:

  • Architecture — Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque, and Soviet all coexist in one walkable city
  • Affordability — significantly cheaper than Western European capitals
  • Food scene — a rapidly evolving restaurant culture blending traditional and modern
  • Nature — the city is 50% green space, and the Neris River runs through the centre
  • Vibe — relaxed, creative, and increasingly cosmopolitan

Did You Know?

Vilnius has more baroque architecture than any other city in Northern Europe, earning it the nickname "The Rome of the North".

docs.title: Top 5 Must-See Spots
docs.text: # Attraction District Why Visit 1 Gediminas Tower Old Town Panoramic city views, symbol of Vilnius 2 Vilnius Cathedral Old Town Stunning neoclassical architecture 3 Užupis Republic Užupis Quirky artist district with its own constitution 4 Gate of Dawn Old Town Most important religious site in the city 5 Bernardine Garden Old Town Beautiful riverside park below the castle hill

docs.title: About This Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Vilnius Travel Guide — an open, community-friendly documentation site built to help travellers explore one of Europe's most underrated capital cities.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This guide aims to be:

  • Practical — real information you can use on the ground
  • Up to date — regularly reviewed and updated
  • Honest — genuine recommendations, not paid placements
  • Accessible — written in plain English for international visitors

docs.title: About Vilnius
docs.text:
Vilnius is the capital of Lithuania, a country of 2.8 million people on the eastern shore of the Baltic Sea. Lithuania was the last country in Europe to officially adopt Christianity (1387), and its capital reflects this unique history — a city where pagan traditions, Catholic baroque, Jewish heritage, Soviet modernism, and contemporary European culture all exist side by side.

Fact Detail Country Lithuania Region Baltic States, Northern Europe EU Member Since 2004 NATO Member Since 2004 Population (city) ~580,000 Area 401 km² Elevation 112 m above sea level Founded ~1323 (by Grand Duke Gediminas) UNESCO status Old Town listed since 1994

docs.title: A Brief History
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[1323 - Founded by Gediminas] --> B[1387 - Capital of Grand Duchy of Lithuania]
    B --> C[1795 - Russian Empire annexation]
    C --> D[1918 - Independence declared]
    D --> E[1940 - Soviet occupation]
    E --> F[1990 - Independence restored]
    F --> G[2004 - EU and NATO membership]
    G --> H[Today - Vibrant European capital]

docs.title: How to Contribute
docs.text:
This site is built with MkDocs and the Material theme, with content written in Markdown. All source files are hosted on GitHub.

If you notice outdated information, incorrect prices, or want to suggest a new restaurant or attraction, contributions are welcome.

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text:
• Open an issue on the GitHub repository
  • Describe what is incorrect or missing
  • Include the page name and section

docs.title: Submitting Changes
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository
  2. Create a branch: git checkout -b fix/page-name
  3. Edit the relevant .md file in the docs/ folder
  4. Submit a pull request with a clear description

docs.title: Built With
docs.text:
Tool Purpose MkDocs Static site generator Material for MkDocs Theme GitHub Pages Hosting Markdown Content format

Disclaimer

All prices, hours, and practical information in this guide are approximate and subject to change. Always verify details directly with venues before visiting.

docs.title: Old Town (Senamiestis)
docs.text:
Vilnius Old Town is the historic heart of the city and one of the largest and best-preserved medieval old towns in Europe, designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994.

Area Facts

  • Size: 3.6 km² — the largest Old Town in the Baltic states
  • UNESCO status: World Heritage Site since 1994
  • Architecture: Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque, Classicism — all in one walk

docs.title: Vilnius Cathedral & Bell Tower
docs.text:
The Cathedral of St Stanislaus and St Vladislaus is the main Roman Catholic cathedral of Lithuania. The neoclassical building dominates Cathedral Square — the symbolic centre of the city.

  • Hours: Mon–Sat 07:00–19:00, Sun 07:00–20:00
  • Entry: Free
  • Tip: Look for the Stebuklas (Miracle) tile in the square — it marks the starting point of the Baltic Way, the 1989 human chain across all three Baltic states.

docs.title: Gediminas Tower
docs.text:
The only remaining part of the Upper Castle, perched on a hill above the Old Town. Offers the best panoramic views of Vilnius.

  • Hours: Daily 10:00–19:00 (summer), 10:00–17:00 (winter)
  • Entry: €5 adults, €2.50 students
  • Access: Walk up (15 min) or take the funicular railway

docs.title: Gate of Dawn (Ausros Vartai)
docs.text:
The only remaining gate of the old city wall. Inside is a small chapel housing a famous image of the Virgin Mary — one of the most important pilgrimage sites in Central and Eastern Europe.

  • Hours: Daily 06:00–19:00
  • Entry: Free
  • Note: Dress modestly when entering the chapel.

docs.title: St Anne's Church
docs.text:
A stunning example of Flamboyant Gothic architecture, reportedly so admired by Napoleon that he wished he could carry it back to Paris in the palm of his hand.

  • Hours: Tue–Sat 10:00–18:00, Sun 10:00–15:00
  • Entry: Free

docs.title: Recommended Walking Route
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Cathedral Square] --> B[Pilies Street]
    B --> C[St Annes Church]
    C --> D[Bernardine Garden]
    D --> E[Uzupis Bridge]
    E --> F[Maironio Street back]
    F --> A

Allow 2–3 hours for a leisurely walk with stops.

docs.title: Tips for Visiting
docs.text:
Do's

  • Explore the courtyards hidden behind the main streets — some of the best surprises are off the beaten path
  • Visit early morning (before 09:00) for atmospheric, crowd-free streets
  • Walk up to Gediminas Tower at sunset for golden-hour views

Don'ts

  • Don't rely on a car — most of the Old Town is pedestrianised
  • Don't skip the side streets — Stikliu, Sv. Mykolo, and Bernardinu are all beautiful

docs.title: Šnipiškės (New City Centre)
docs.text:
Šnipiškės, located just across the Neris River from the Old Town, is Vilnius's modern business district — a striking contrast of glass skyscrapers, contemporary architecture, and riverside promenades.

Quick Facts

  • Location: North bank of the Neris River, opposite the Old Town
  • Character: Business, modern, cosmopolitan
  • Best for: Skyline views, modern restaurants, rooftop bars

docs.title: Why Visit
docs.text: While most tourists stay in the Old Town, Šnipiškės is worth crossing the river for. From here you get the best views of Vilnius Cathedral and the Old Town skyline, especially at sunset. The district has also developed a strong restaurant and bar scene in recent years.

docs.title: Vilnius Skyline Viewpoint
docs.text: The riverbank promenade on the north side of the Neris offers a perfect view of the Old Town and Cathedral across the water. Particularly stunning at golden hour.

docs.title: Europa Tower
docs.text:
At 148 metres, it was the tallest building in the Baltic states when completed in 2004. The observation deck on the 26th floor offers 360° views of the city.

  • Hours: Mon–Fri 09:00–18:00
  • Entry: €5

docs.title: Panorama Shopping Centre
docs.text: The largest shopping mall in Lithuania, with over 200 shops, a cinema, and dining options. Useful for rainy days.

docs.title: Restaurants & Bars
docs.text:
Šnipiškės has developed into one of the best areas for contemporary dining in Vilnius.

Venue Type Notes Apvalus Stulas Fine dining Tasting menus, panoramic views Stekai Steakhouse Best steaks in Vilnius Skybar Rooftop bar 22nd floor, city views Laumės Juosta Casual Good brunch spot

docs.title: Getting There
docs.text:
From the Old Town, cross the Mindaugas Bridge (a 10-minute walk from Cathedral Square) or take any bus heading north across the Neris.

Evening Visit

Come at sunset and walk back across the bridge to the Old Town — the view of the lit-up Cathedral and tower is one of Vilnius's best sights.

docs.title: Užupis
docs.text:
Užupis (meaning "beyond the river" in Lithuanian) is Vilnius's most eccentric and beloved neighbourhood. On April 1, 1997, it declared itself an independent republic — complete with its own president, army (of about 12 people), currency, and a constitution.

The Republic of Užupis

  • Declared independent: April 1, 1997
  • Population: ~7,000
  • President: Romas Lileikis (artist and filmmaker)
  • National holiday: April 1 (April Fools' Day)
  • Constitution: Posted on the street in 23 languages

docs.title: The Constitution
docs.text:
The Užupis Constitution is one of the neighbourhood's great delights. It is engraved on mirrored plaques on Paupio Street in dozens of languages. Some memorable articles:

Article 1: Everyone has the right to live by the River Vilnelė, and the River Vilnelė has the right to flow by everyone.

Article 12: Everyone has the right to be idle.

Article 41: Everyone has the right to be happy.

Article 42: Everyone is not obliged to be happy.

Find the plaques

Walk along Paupio Street beside the small Vilnelė river to read the full constitution. It's one of the most charming walks in Vilnius.

docs.title: Angel of Užupis
docs.text: The neighbourhood's symbol — a bronze trumpet-playing angel on a column at the main intersection. Cast in 2002, it represents freedom and the creative spirit of the area.

docs.title: Galleries & Studios
docs.text:
Užupis is packed with independent art galleries, artist studios, and craft workshops. Many are open to visitors. Look out for:

  • The Užupis Art Incubator
  • Small photography galleries on the back streets
  • Ceramic and textile workshops

docs.title: Cafes & Bars
docs.text: The neighbourhood has a relaxed, bohemian cafe culture. Grab a coffee, sit by the river, and watch the world go by.

docs.title: Crossing the Border
docs.text:
On April 1st (the Republic's national holiday), the Užupis bridges become "border crossings" where guards stamp your passport with the Užupis stamp. Highly recommended if you happen to be in Vilnius on that date.

"Border" Bridge Location Užupio tiltas Main entry from Old Town Malūnų tiltas Southern entry

docs.title: Getting There
docs.text:
Užupis is a 10-minute walk from Cathedral Square. Walk east through the Old Town along Pilies Street, then turn left past St Anne's Church and cross the small Vilnelė river.

Best Time to Visit

Late afternoon on a weekday — the light on the river is beautiful, the streets are quiet, and you can browse the galleries without crowds.

docs.title: Cafes & Coffee
docs.text:
Vilnius has a thriving, serious coffee culture. The city is packed with excellent independent specialty cafes, and the standard of espresso-based drinks is genuinely high. You will not struggle to find a great cup of coffee here.

Coffee Culture

Vilnius baristas regularly compete and win at European-level barista championships. The city takes its coffee seriously.

docs.title: Crooked Nose & Coffee Stories
docs.text:
The flagship of Vilnius's specialty coffee scene. Multiple locations, consistently excellent espresso and filter coffee. The Old Town branch is a great place to start the day.

  • Best for: Espresso, pour-over, single origins
  • Location: Pilies g. 22 (Old Town) + other branches
  • Vibe: Modern, friendly, knowledgeable staff
  • Price: €€

docs.title: Caffeine
docs.text:
A beloved local chain that helped establish the specialty coffee movement in Vilnius. Excellent beans, beautiful spaces, and very good food menus.

  • Best for: Flat whites, brunch, working remotely
  • Location: Multiple locations across the city
  • Vibe: Relaxed, good for laptops, always busy
  • Price: €€

docs.title: Finger's Coffee
docs.text:
Small, intimate specialty cafe near the Old Town. Takes its single-origin filter coffee very seriously.

  • Best for: Filter coffee, quiet mornings
  • Location: Saviciaus g. 10
  • Vibe: Quiet, focused, coffee-forward
  • Price: €€

docs.title: Kava Kahve Cafe
docs.text:
A cosy, eclectic cafe in the Old Town with one of the longest coffee menus in the city. Also serves good light food.

  • Best for: Variety, atmosphere, people-watching
  • Location: Sv. Kazimiero g. 3, Old Town
  • Vibe: Warm, eclectic, characterful
  • Price: €

docs.title: Cafes with a View or Atmosphere
Šv. Kazimiero Kavinė
docs.text: Set in a beautiful courtyard of the Church of St Casimir. A peaceful spot for coffee and cake away from the main tourist streets.

docs.title: Užupio Kavinė
docs.text:
Perched right on the Vilnelė riverbank in Užupis. Perfect in summer — sit outside and watch the river while drinking coffee or local beer.

  • Location: Užupio g. 2
  • Best in: Summer, sunny days
  • Vibe: Bohemian, relaxed, artistic crowd

docs.title: What to Order
docs.text:
Espresso-basedFilter / Pour-overCold DrinksWith Food

Lithuanian cafes follow Italian espresso traditions. Order a flat white, cortado, or cappuccino for the best expression of the coffee. Milk quality is generally very high.

Specialty cafes all offer filter options. Ask for the single origin of the day at Crooked Nose or Finger's — staff will happily explain the origin and tasting notes.

Cold brew is widely available in summer. Matcha lattes have become very popular in Vilnius cafes. Freshly squeezed juices are also common.

Most Vilnius cafes serve excellent food alongside coffee — particularly good for breakfast and brunch. Look for ryžiai su daržovėmis (rice with vegetables) or locally baked pastries.

docs.title: Cafe Hours & Tips
docs.text:
Opening Hours

Most independent cafes open around 08:00–09:00 and close between 20:00–22:00. Sunday hours are often shorter. Always check Google Maps for current hours.

Remote Working

Vilnius cafes are very laptop-friendly. Caffeine and Crooked Nose both have good Wi-Fi and plenty of seating. Weekday mornings are the quietest time to work.

docs.title: Lithuanian Cuisine
docs.text:
Lithuanian cuisine is hearty, honest, and deeply tied to the country's agricultural traditions. Shaped by cold winters, forest foraging, and centuries of peasant cooking, it centers on potatoes, rye bread, dairy, pork, and wild mushrooms.

Key Ingredients

  • Potatoes — the cornerstone of Lithuanian cooking
  • Rye bread (ruginė duona) — dark, dense, and delicious
  • Dairy — sour cream (grietinė) appears on almost everything
  • Pork — the most common meat
  • Wild mushrooms & berries — foraged from Lithuanian forests

docs.title: Cepelinai (Zeppelins)
docs.text:
The national dish. Large dumplings made from grated and mashed potatoes, stuffed with minced pork and served with sour cream and bacon bits. Named after the Zeppelin airship due to their shape.

Where to try it

Almost every traditional Lithuanian restaurant serves cepelinai. Try Etno Dvaras in the Old Town for a classic version.

docs.title: Šaltibarščiai (Cold Beet Soup)
docs.text:
A vibrant pink cold soup made from kefir, beetroot, cucumber, and dill. A beloved summer dish, always served with hot boiled potatoes on the side.

  • Best enjoyed: May–September
  • Colour: Unmistakably pink — you will recognise it immediately

docs.title: Kibinai
docs.text:
Pastry pockets filled with meat (usually lamb or pork), originally from the Karaite minority of Trakai. Crispy, buttery, and deeply satisfying.

Day Trip Connection

Kibinai are strongly associated with Trakai (45 min from Vilnius) — a highly recommended day trip where you can eat kibinai at the lakeside.

docs.title: Juoda duona (Dark Rye Bread)
docs.text: Lithuanian rye bread is unlike anything you'll find in Western Europe — dense, slightly sour, and intensely flavoured. Often served with butter, lard, or garlic.

docs.title: Skilandis
docs.text: A traditional smoked sausage made from minced pork and spices, cold-smoked and dried. A staple of Lithuanian charcuterie boards.

docs.title: Kugelis
docs.text: A baked potato pudding — dense, savoury, and filling. Made from grated potatoes, eggs, onions, and bacon. Comfort food at its finest.

docs.title: Beer (Alus)
docs.text: Lithuania has a strong craft beer culture. Local breweries like Vilkmergės, Gubernija, and Dundulis produce excellent beers. The Old Town has numerous craft beer bars.

docs.title: Midus (Mead)
docs.text: Traditional Lithuanian honey wine. Slightly sweet, mildly alcoholic, and very much worth trying. Available at most traditional restaurants.

docs.title: Kefir (Kefyras)
docs.text: Lithuanians drink a lot of kefir — a fermented milk drink. Pick some up at a supermarket; it is cheap, healthy, and a genuine part of local daily life.

docs.title: Dietary Notes
docs.text:
VegetariansVegansGluten-free

Lithuanian cuisine is traditionally meat-heavy, but Vilnius has a growing vegetarian and vegan restaurant scene. Most modern restaurants offer vegetarian options. Šaltibarščiai (cold beet soup) is naturally vegetarian.

Dedicated vegan restaurants exist in Vilnius (try Radharane or Vero Cafe). Traditional cuisine is harder to navigate — look for mushroom dishes, salads, and rye bread sides.

Challenging in traditional restaurants as rye bread and potato dumplings are staples. Modern restaurants increasingly offer gluten-free options — always ask staff directly.

docs.title: Best Restaurants
docs.text:
Vilnius has a rapidly evolving restaurant scene that punches well above its size. From traditional Lithuanian cuisine to creative modern cooking, the city offers excellent value compared to Western European capitals.

Booking Advice

For mid-range and fine dining restaurants, book in advance, especially for Friday and Saturday evenings. Most restaurants accept reservations via their website or by phone.

docs.title: Etno Dvaras
docs.text:
The most reliable spot for traditional Lithuanian food in the Old Town. Rustic wooden interior, generous portions, and a menu that covers all the classics.

  • Location: Pilies g. 16, Old Town
  • Price range: €€
  • Must order: Cepelinai, kugelis, dark rye bread basket
  • Hours: Daily 11:00–23:00

docs.title: Lokys (The Bear)
docs.text:
One of the oldest restaurants in Vilnius, operating since 1972 in a beautiful Gothic cellar. Specialises in game — wild boar, beaver, and venison alongside Lithuanian classics.

  • Location: Stikliu g. 8, Old Town
  • Price range: €€€
  • Must order: Wild boar stew, hunter's platter
  • Hours: Daily 12:00–24:00

docs.title: Senoji Kibininė
docs.text:
The best place in Vilnius for kibinai — the flaky Karaite pastries. Simple, casual, and excellent value.

  • Location: Bernardinu g. 9, Old Town
  • Price range: €
  • Must order: Lamb kibinas, honey beer
  • Hours: Daily 10:00–21:00

docs.title: Amandus
docs.text:
One of Vilnius's top fine dining restaurants, serving creative Lithuanian cuisine with high-quality seasonal ingredients. Elegant but not stuffy.

  • Location: Uzupio g. 20, Uzupis
  • Price range: €€€€
  • Must order: Tasting menu (changes seasonally)
  • Hours: Tue–Sat 18:00–23:00

docs.title: Sweet Root
docs.text:
Consistently ranked among the best restaurants in the Baltics. Creative, artistic tasting menus that showcase Lithuanian ingredients in unexpected ways.

  • Location: Uzupio g. 22, Uzupis
  • Price range: €€€€
  • Must order: Chef's tasting menu (advance booking essential)
  • Hours: Tue–Sat 18:00–22:00

docs.title: Nineteen18
docs.text:
Modern European cuisine in a beautifully designed space. Great cocktail bar attached.

  • Location: Gedimino pr. 13, New Town
  • Price range: €€€
  • Hours: Mon–Sat 12:00–23:00

docs.title: Halės Turgus (Hales Market)
docs.text:
Vilnius's main covered market, a short walk from the Old Town. Fresh produce, hot food stalls, and local snacks at very low prices. A great place to eat like a local.

  • Location: Pylimo g. 58
  • Price range: €
  • Hours: Mon–Sat 07:00–18:00, Sun 07:00–15:00

docs.title: Snekutis
docs.text:
A beloved neighbourhood bar and canteen serving cheap, hearty Lithuanian food and local beer. Cash only, no frills, completely authentic.

  • Location: Sv. Stepono g. 8, Uzupis
  • Price range: €
  • Hours: Mon–Thu 11:00–22:00, Fri–Sat 11:00–24:00

docs.title: Price Guide
docs.text: Symbol Price per person (food only) € Under €10 €€ €10–25 €€€ €25–50 €€€€ €50+

docs.title: Restaurant Map Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Old Town] --> B[Etno Dvaras - Traditional]
    A --> C[Lokys - Game & Traditional]
    A --> D[Senoji Kibinine - Kibinai]
    E[Uzupis] --> F[Amandus - Fine Dining]
    E --> G[Sweet Root - Fine Dining]
    E --> H[Snekutis - Budget]
    I[New Town] --> J[Nineteen18 - Modern European]
    K[Hales Market] --> L[Various Budget Stalls]

docs.title: Best Time to Visit
docs.text: Vilnius has four distinct seasons, each offering a different experience of the city.

docs.title: Season by Season
docs.text:
☀️ Summer (Jun–Aug)🍂 Autumn (Sep–Nov)❄️ Winter (Dec–Feb)🌸 Spring (Mar–May)

The peak season. Long days (up to 17 hours of daylight), outdoor terraces packed, festivals in full swing.

  • Average temperature: 18–24°C
  • Crowds: High
  • Prices: Highest
  • Highlights: Vilnius Street Music Day (June), outdoor cinema, beer gardens

Best for

First-time visitors, outdoor lovers, festival-goers.

A hidden favourite. The city turns gold and amber, crowds thin out, and prices drop. September is particularly lovely.

  • Average temperature: 5–15°C
  • Crowds: Low–Medium
  • Prices: Lower
  • Highlights: Vilnius City Days (September), mushroom season, cosy cafes

Local Tip

September is arguably the best month — summer warmth lingers, the Old Town is beautiful in early autumn colour, and tourists are gone.

A fairy-tale atmosphere. The Christmas market in Cathedral Square is magical. Cold, but rarely extreme.

  • Average temperature: -5 to +2°C
  • Crowds: Low (except Christmas/New Year)
  • Prices: Lowest
  • Highlights: Christmas Market (Dec), ice skating, cosy restaurant season

Expect

Short days (as few as 7.5 hours of daylight), possible snow and ice on cobblestones — pack warm layers and good boots.

The city wakes up. Trees blossom, terraces reopen, and the city feels energetic. May is excellent.

  • Average temperature: 5–15°C
  • Crowds: Low–Medium
  • Prices: Mid-range
  • Highlights: Easter celebrations, Kaziukas Fair (March), Botanical Garden bloom

docs.title: Average Weather by Month
docs.text: Month Avg High (°C) Avg Low (°C) Rain Days Notes January 0 -6 10 Coldest month, possible snow February 1 -6 9 Still cold, days getting longer March 7 -1 10 Kaziukas craft fair April 13 4 11 Warming up, cherry blossoms May 19 8 12 Excellent — warm and green June 22 12 13 Peak season begins July 24 14 13 Hottest month August 23 13 12 Still summer, slightly quieter September 18 9 12 Best month overall October 11 4 12 Autumn colours peak November 4 0 13 Grey, but atmospheric December 1 -4 12 Christmas market magic

docs.title: Getting Around
docs.text: Vilnius is a compact city and very easy to navigate. The Old Town is almost entirely walkable, and public transport covers the rest efficiently.

docs.title: From the Airport
docs.text:
Vilnius Airport (VNO) is located just 7 km south of the city centre — one of the most conveniently located airports in Europe.

Option Duration Cost Notes Train 7 min €0.65 Runs every 30 min, most convenient Bus (No. 1, 2) 30–40 min €1 Slower but runs more frequently Bolt / Uber 15–20 min €8–12 Door to door, best late at night Taxi 15–20 min €15–25 Use only licensed taxis

Best Option

The train is the fastest and cheapest option. Buy tickets at the station kiosk or via the Trafi app.

docs.title: Walking
docs.text: The Old Town is best explored on foot. Most major sights are within a 20-minute walk of Cathedral Square. Wear comfortable shoes — the cobblestones can be uneven.

docs.title: Public Transport
docs.text:
Vilnius has an extensive bus and trolleybus network.

  • Fare: €1.00 (cash on board) or €0.65 (with an M-Ticket card)
  • Day pass: €3.00
  • Operating hours: ~05:30–23:00 (night buses on weekends)
  • App: Download Trafi for real-time routes and schedules

docs.title: Bolt Scooters
docs.text:
Electric scooters are available throughout the city via the Bolt app. A fun and cheap way to cover medium distances.

  • Unlock fee: €0.50
  • Per minute: €0.15

docs.title: CycloCity Bike Share
docs.text:
Vilnius has a well-developed cycling infrastructure.

  • Available at 60+ stations across the city
  • First 30 minutes free with a day pass (€2)
  • Great for riverside routes along the Neris

docs.title: Car Rental & Taxis
docs.text:
Driving in Old Town

Most of the Old Town is a restricted traffic zone. You need a permit to drive there. For visiting the Old Town, always use public transport or walk.

  • Licensed taxis are metered. Starting fare ~€1.30, ~€0.80/km
  • Always use Bolt or Uber apps to avoid overcharging

docs.title: Key Routes
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Airport VNO] -->|Train 7 min| B[Vilnius Train Station]
    B -->|Walk 15 min| C[Cathedral Square]
    C -->|Walk 10 min| D[Gediminas Tower]
    C -->|Walk 5 min| E[Old Town Streets]
    C -->|Walk 20 min| F[Uzupis]
    B -->|Bus 15 min| G[Shnipishkes CBD]

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This page covers everything you need to know before your trip to Vilnius — visas, money, language, and practical essentials.

docs.title: Entry Requirements
docs.text:
EU / EEA CitizensUS / UK / CA / AU CitizensOther Countries

No visa required. A valid national ID card or passport is sufficient. Lithuania is part of the Schengen Area.

No visa required for stays up to 90 days within any 180-day period. A valid passport is required.

Check the Lithuanian Ministry of Foreign Affairs website for visa requirements specific to your nationality.

Passport Validity

Your passport should be valid for at least 3 months beyond your planned departure date from the Schengen Area.

docs.title: Money & Budget
docs.text:
Lithuania uses the Euro (€). Vilnius is affordable compared to most Western European capitals.

Budget Level Daily Spend (approx.) What You Get Budget €40–60 Hostel dorm, cheap eats, free sights Mid-range €80–130 3-star hotel, restaurant meals, paid attractions Comfort €150–250+ Boutique hotel, fine dining, guided tours

ATMs

ATMs are widely available throughout the city. Use bank ATMs (Swedbank, SEB, Luminor) to avoid high fees from independent ATM operators. Most restaurants and shops accept card payments.

docs.title: Language
docs.text:
The official language is Lithuanian, one of the oldest living Indo-European languages. However, English is widely spoken, especially by younger people and in tourist areas.

Useful Lithuanian phrases:

Lithuanian Pronunciation English Labas lah-bahs Hello Ačiū ah-choo Thank you Prašom prah-shom Please / You're welcome Atsiprašau aht-see-prah-show Excuse me / Sorry Kiek kainuoja? kyek kai-nuo-yah How much does it cost?

docs.title: Connectivity
docs.text:
• SIM cards — available at the airport and convenience stores (Tele2, Bite, Telia). A prepaid SIM with 10GB data costs around €5–10.
  • Wi-Fi — free Wi-Fi is available in most cafes, restaurants, hotels, and public spaces including the Old Town.

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
Vilnius is a very safe city for tourists. Violent crime is rare. As in any city, take standard precautions:

Safe Practices

  • Keep an eye on your belongings in crowded areas
  • Use licensed taxis or Bolt/Uber apps
  • Avoid poorly lit streets in unfamiliar areas late at night

docs.title: Useful Apps
docs.text:
• Bolt — the dominant ride-hailing app (cheaper than taxis)
  • Trafi — public transport routes and real-time schedules
  • Google Maps — works excellently for navigation in Vilnius
  • Vilnius – the city in your pocket — official city guide app
